lines.text: "Can't be unseen. Can't be unthought.

It’s about irreversible contact. Trauma. Maybe. Once your mind has genuinely encountered something larger—whether truth, horror, beauty, or a new way of seeing yourself—you don’t get to neatly roll back to the previous version. Enlightenment and contamination can look disturbingly similar.
Let's start with the chapter. Rebound. The Dáinn rebounding back to Mairee, sure. But also Kirsten rebounding from grief by hurling herself at the next crisis. Even the Hopper literally rebounds.
Westhoff says: "Like a resisting pawn forced back into formation against its will." Nyland plays with 'agency' again.
Let's start with Kirsten's screams reaching the camp before she does. She doesn't stop at the washing station. Slams the door. Throws a crate at the wall.
Sarah's instinct is fascinating here. She knows Kirsten's torn. Their relationship too. Sarah held her back from going after Nasr.

What actually breaks Kirsten, and myself to be honest, was the moment Sarah takes Kirsten's trembling hand and presses it to her own chest. "My heart is racing. I'm scared. I need you." Sarah says, disarming her by being vulnerable, not by being right.
It's so raw.
Then, Marco Thieman. We've never met this guy before. He's the leader of the other rangers, gearing up to go deeper. And his line is raw too— "Before long, she will eat us all."
Casually announcing his own funeral on the way out the door."
Thieman already gets it. Well, not it. *Her*.
And she gives them not time to catch their breath. DROP 7 calls in. Broken bones. Torn pumps. Stripped flesh.
And the Hopper.
The most insane shabby piece of equipment. I want one.

A flying flatbed truck. Five meters square. No cockpit. Two fold-out seats and magnetic clamps. You strap in, the klaxon goes twice, and you get fired across the jungle like a cannonball. Sarah's just like, "Strap in tight."
And then Jimenez — Jimenez! — sprints across the pad, ignores the klaxon, and vaults into the thing mid-launch. Like a loyal pup desperate not to be left behind.
It earns him two shit-clicks though. Well, one for calling Strand "Ma'am". Nyland keeps finding humor in the cracks.

DROP 7 itself is one of the doomed outposts. The first signs of collapse. A station expected to deliver twenty percent of the colony's water. And the Centurion, Jax, won't even bother sending aid. He wants Kirsten to fail there.
Then we shift hard, mid-chapter, to the Dáinn. Westhoff, Jia, Beltrán, Gail. And we get one of the best strategy scenes in the book so far, because nobody's making the obvious move.
It's four exhausted, gravity-sick people. Beltrán can barely sit upright. This is the resistance, and the resistance is already running on fumes.
A resistance that chooses not to resist! Jia wants to rally the haulers and shut Mairee down from orbit. Ben says no. "If we give up our humanity, we're already extinct."
What a line. But is it naive?
Well. Gail isn't. I'm not sure what to do with Gail yet. But he's the one in the room willing to ask out loud: what if Corbin's not wrong?

Devil's advocate, he calls himself. Smirking. He says supply lines are gone, hysteria is coming. Either Gail is testing the room, or he's already halfway to the other side. He's a logistician too.  "He'll use levers you won't, Captain. You know what I mean."
That's the whole problem with fighting clean against someone who isn't.
And the unconventional plan that emerges is so good. Dom again. The Nulgee. Surrender the Dáinn. Let Ben be relieved of command. Let Corbin think he's won.
Hold on — Nulgee. "You Nulgees. Always insane, always brilliant." What does it actually mean?
I think it is a reference to those living most of their lives in zero gravity. Zero-Gee. Null-Gee.
I love that Beltrán is the architect of the resistance but can't even sit upright. He operates at the center of the ship. Like a spider weaving the web.
Corbin thinks he's the spider in his own mind, controlling the Grid. But Beltrán flips it — Corbin is the fly walking into his web.

And the Muro question is the most dangerous piece. All comms pass through The Ears. Beltrán says he's already in touch. But Jia's instinct lands hard: "Maybe they're on to us. Maybe that's why Corbin felt confident using Vilde as leverage.
Two storylines that have been running in parallel for the entire book just got a line drawn between them. Kaspar Savannen. The COG. Grid. Egg. Olly.
So many characters. But there's a thread. And I'm hooked.
